summary: The unsolved_math dataset contains 2084 open mathematical problems across 12 curated sets, with the sampled subset showing exclusively resolved issues — every listed problem (um00001–um00047) has 0 open and 1 resolved issue, all titled as 'Proof:' submissions. The proof status distribution is uniform, with q1 through q10 each holding exactly 100 issues, suggesting a systematic batching or automated ingestion rather than organic research traction. This uniformity warrants verification before drawing conclusions about genuine progress.
problems: All sampled problems (um00001–um00047) report 1 resolved issue and 0 open issues, which is statistically implausible for genuine open problems like P vs NP and the Riemann Hypothesis — likely indicates false-positive 'Proof:' submissions or auto-closed issues.
Perfectly uniform proof-status buckets (q1–q10 each at exactly 100 issues) strongly suggest synthetic, paginated, or programmatically generated data rather than real research activity.
All resolved issue titles follow an identical 'Proof: <problem name>' template, a pattern consistent with spam, AI-generated proof claims, or bulk placeholder entries.
No genuinely open issues are visible in the provided slice, making it impossible to assess real mathematical traction or community discussion.
Famous unsolved problems (Millennium Prize, Hilbert) appearing as 'resolved' contradicts their known open status, indicating a data-integrity or labeling error.
highlights: The dataset is comprehensive in scope, spanning 2084 problems across 12 prestigious curated sets including Millennium Prize, Hilbert's 23, and the full Erdős collection.
Issue tracking and proof-status categorization are in place, providing a structured foundation for monitoring once data quality is validated.
Clear, consistent title conventions make automated triage and filtering straightforward to implement.

suggested_todos.title: Audit and verify the validity of all 'Proof:' resolved issues against problems known to remain open (e.g., Riemann Hypothesis, P vs NP)
Investigate why every sampled problem shows exactly 0 open / 1 resolved and whether issues are being auto-closed incorrectly
Validate the uniform q1–q10 (100 each) proof-status distribution to rule out synthetic or paginated data artifacts
Implement a spam/false-claim filter for unverified 'Proof:' submissions before they are marked resolved
Generate a corrected report once true open-issue counts are restored to identify real high-traction problems
